hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The properties at 219 and 225 Augusta Avenue each have value as a nineteenth-century House- form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through ground floor alterations. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as the outside two of a pair of semi-detached residences, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including a cross-gabled roofline, brick cladding and fenestration. Contextually, the property is important in maintaining and supporting the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 19. 219 Augusta Avenue N/A 20. 225 Augusta Avenue N/A 26 DCH | FO :D 230 Augusta Avenue 230 AUGUSTA AVENUE

facade_description: 228 Augusta Ave presents a converted house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its converted house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. HCD note: The properties at 219 and 225 Augusta Avenue each have value as a nineteenth-century House- form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through ground floor alterations. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as the outside two of a pair of semi-detached residences, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including a cross-gabled roofline, brick cladding and fenestration. Contextually, the property is important in maintaining and supporting the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
